Q1: My study routine was honestly not as organized as I would have liked. I typically tried to dedicate about 20 hours a week to studying, but it often felt scattered. I'm a visual learner, so I spent a lot of time watching lecture videos and creating my own diagrams and notes. Some weeks I'd be really focused, other weeks the anxiety would get to me and I'd struggle to concentrate even when I was sitting with my materials.
Q2: My pattern varied quite a bit depending on what was due. I tried to have some consistency, but with 8 online courses to juggle, I often found myself shifting focus based on which deadline was most pressing. When things piled up, I'd have to abandon my preferred schedule and just do whatever was most urgent. It wasn't ideal, but that's how I survived.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check assignment deadlines or watch a lecture video. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about something and that would prompt me to log in. To be honest, I didn't log in just to browse or explore – it was always purpose-driven, like checking what was due or downloading materials I needed.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more regularly, but it always felt like a tool I used when necessary rather than something integrated into my daily life. I preferred to download what I needed and work offline with my own notes and visual aids.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me because I learn best visually. I watched over 192 minutes of video content because seeing concepts explained visually helped them stick. I also found the assignment submission portal helpful just for keeping track of what I'd completed. Having visual representations of concepts made everything clearer for me.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums – I only posted once the entire course. I found them overwhelming and didn't feel like I had much to contribute. The anxiety made it hard to put my thoughts out there for everyone to see. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because I was already stretched thin with my core materials.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to what I'd be tested on or what I needed for assignments. Visual content like videos and diagrams always felt valuable to me. If something was text-heavy or felt disconnected from the main learning objectives, I'd often deprioritize it because I only had so much energy to give.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. With my anxiety levels being what they were, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. Required activities came with clear consequences, so they got done first. Optional ones often got pushed aside, even when I knew they might help, because the pressure of mandatory work was always looming.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded lecture slides and videos to study offline constantly. I'd create my own visual notes, diagrams, and mind maps based on the course content. I also used YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't fully understand from the lectures. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just me with my notes and colored pens making things visual.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd download a PDF once and then spend hours over several days annotating it, creating diagrams, and reviewing it. Or I'd watch a lecture video once online but replay parts of it from my downloaded copy multiple times. My offline study time was significant but completely invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach actually. I'd access materials once on the LMS, download everything I needed, and then continue studying them offline for days or even weeks. It was more convenient for me to have everything on my laptop where I could organize it my way. I'd create visual summaries and study those repeatedly without ever logging back in.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and everything became harder. Around exam time especially, I'd feel paralyzed even though I knew I needed to study. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped somewhat, but there were still days when I'd sit at my desk for hours accomplishing very little because my mind was racing with worry.
Q13: Managing 8 online courses was genuinely overwhelming at times. When multiple deadlines converged, I'd have to make difficult choices about what to prioritize. Fatigue was real – some days I was just too mentally exhausted to engage meaningfully with anything. My anxiety would spike during these periods, making it even harder to focus on the LMS or any coursework.
Q14: Yes, definitely. Sometimes I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my mind was elsewhere. The anxiety would make it hard to actually absorb what I was looking at. I'd watch a video but realize at the end that I hadn't retained anything because I was too worried about other assignments or life things. Those sessions felt wasted even though they showed up as activity.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they triggered my anxiety. The idea of putting my thoughts out there for others to judge was paralyzing. I also avoided starting on bigger assignments until closer to deadlines because the scope felt overwhelming. Even knowing they were important, the anxiety made it easier to focus on smaller, less intimidating tasks first.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator for LMS activity. I'd log in more frequently as deadlines approached, sometimes cramming a lot of activity into short periods. My assignment completion rate of about 77% reflects that I did engage when things were due, but I wasn't proactive about getting ahead. Deadlines created urgency that cut through my usual anxiety-driven avoidance.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though not always positively. Good feedback on assignments boosted my mood and made me more willing to engage with the next task. But when feedback was critical or I got a lower grade than expected, it would spike my anxiety and sometimes make me avoid the LMS for a while. It was a complicated relationship.
Q18: The course structure with 8 online courses simultaneously made it challenging to give each one proper attention. I had to constantly triage what needed my focus most urgently. The structure of having videos available was helpful for my visual learning style, but the sheer volume of content across all courses meant I couldn't engage deeply with everything.
Q19: Having video content available was definitely helpful for me as a visual learner. What made engagement harder was when courses relied heavily on text-based discussions or readings without visual aids. The lack of clear visual roadmaps for what to study when also made it harder – I sometimes felt lost about what to prioritize among all the available materials.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or overwhelmed like me. You might log in multiple times, start watching videos, open documents, but never actually complete or submit anything because you're stuck in a cycle of worry. Or maybe they're exploring, trying to understand what's expected, but can't bring themselves to commit to producing work. The activity shows effort, just not productive output.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My assignment scores were pretty good – around 87% – even though my LMS engagement looked low. That's because I did a lot of learning offline with my downloaded materials and visual notes. Someone might appear inactive but be studying effectively in ways the system can't track. The LMS doesn't see the hours I spent with my hand-drawn diagrams.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in and what they click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I was anxiously scrolling without absorbing anything or deeply focused. They also miss all offline studying, which for me was the majority of my actual learning. The emotional state behind the clicks is invisible.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively creating visual representations of concepts, asking myself questions, and connecting ideas together. It's a mental state of curiosity and focus, which honestly was hard for me to maintain consistently given my anxiety. True engagement feels different from just completing tasks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels and how much energy it took just to open my laptop some days. It wouldn't show the mindfulness exercises I did to calm myself before studying, or the hours I spent with offline visual notes. It also wouldn't capture that despite my struggles and failing grade, I genuinely tried and cared about learning. My effort was real even when the results didn't show it.
